rules.rule_statement: The program shall contain no violations of the standard C syntax and constraints, and shall not exceed the implementation’s translation limits
rules.description: This code violates Rule 1.1 because the program shall contain no violations of the standard C syntax and constraints, and shall not exceed the implementation’s translation limits. Violating core language and implementation rules can make the program non-portable, non-compliant, or undefined.

rules.rule_statement: Language extensions should not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 1.2 because language extensions should not be used. Violating core language and implementation rules can make the program non-portable, non-compliant, or undefined.
rules.violated_code: __interrupt void isr(void) { }   /* language extension */

rules.rule_statement: There shall be no occurrence of undefined or critical unspecified behaviour
rules.description: This code violates Rule 1.3 because there shall be no occurrence of undefined or critical unspecified behaviour. Violating core language and implementation rules can make the program non-portable, non-compliant, or undefined.
rules.violated_code: int x = 1 / 0;   /* undefined behaviour */

rules.rule_statement: A project shall not contain unreachable code
rules.description: This code violates Rule 2.1 because a project shall not contain unreachable code. Unused, dead, or unreachable constructs increase maintenance cost and can hide real defects.

rules.rule_statement: There shall be no dead code
rules.description: This code violates Rule 2.2 because there shall be no dead code. Unused, dead, or unreachable constructs increase maintenance cost and can hide real defects.

rules.rule_statement: A project should not contain unused type declarations
rules.description: This code violates Rule 2.3 because a project should not contain unused type declarations. Unused, dead, or unreachable constructs increase maintenance cost and can hide real defects.
rules.violated_code:
typedef unsigned int speed_t; /* unused type */
void f(void) { }

rules.rule_statement: A project should not contain unused tag declarations
rules.description: This code violates Rule 2.4 because a project should not contain unused tag declarations. Unused, dead, or unreachable constructs increase maintenance cost and can hide real defects.
rules.violated_code:
struct sensor_tag { int v; }; /* unused tag */
void f(void) { }

rules.rule_statement: A project should not contain unused macro declarations
rules.description: This code violates Rule 2.5 because a project should not contain unused macro declarations. Unused, dead, or unreachable constructs increase maintenance cost and can hide real defects.
rules.violated_code:
#define UNUSED_LIMIT 10
void f(void) { }

rules.rule_statement: A function should not contain unused label declarations
rules.description: This code violates Rule 2.6 because a function should not contain unused label declarations. Unused, dead, or unreachable constructs increase maintenance cost and can hide real defects.

rules.rule_statement: There should be no unused parameters in functions
rules.description: This code violates Rule 2.7 because there should be no unused parameters in functions. Unused, dead, or unreachable constructs increase maintenance cost and can hide real defects.

rules.rule_statement: The character sequences /* and // shall not be used within a comment
rules.description: This code violates Rule 3.1 because the character sequences /* and // shall not be used within a comment. Ambiguous comment content can mislead reviewers and static analysis tools.
rules.violated_code:
/* outer comment with // and /* nested marker */
int x = 0;

rules.rule_statement: Line-splicing shall not be used in // comments
rules.description: This code violates Rule 3.2 because line-splicing shall not be used in // comments. Ambiguous comment content can mislead reviewers and static analysis tools.

rules.rule_statement: Octal and hexadecimal escape sequences shall be terminated
rules.description: This code violates Rule 4.1 because octal and hexadecimal escape sequences shall be terminated. Non-portable lexical forms reduce readability and may be interpreted differently by tools or compilers.
rules.violated_code: char c = '\x1f1';   /* escape sequence not terminated clearly */

rules.rule_statement: Trigraphs shall not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 4.2 because trigraphs shall not be used. Non-portable lexical forms reduce readability and may be interpreted differently by tools or compilers.
rules.violated_code:
??=define VALUE 1
int x = VALUE;   /* trigraph used */

rules.rule_statement: External identifiers shall be distinct
rules.description: This code violates Rule 5.1 because external identifiers shall be distinct. Poor identifier hygiene creates confusion, shadowing, or name collisions across the codebase.

rules.rule_statement: Identifiers declared in the same scope and name space shall be distinct
rules.description: This code violates Rule 5.2 because identifiers declared in the same scope and name space shall be distinct. Poor identifier hygiene creates confusion, shadowing, or name collisions across the codebase.

rules.rule_statement: An identifier declared in an inner scope shall not hide an identifier declared in an outer scope
rules.description: This code violates Rule 5.3 because an identifier declared in an inner scope shall not hide an identifier declared in an outer scope. Poor identifier hygiene creates confusion, shadowing, or name collisions across the codebase.

rules.rule_statement: A macro identifier shall be distinct
rules.description: This code violates Rule 5.4 because a macro identifier shall be distinct. Poor identifier hygiene creates confusion, shadowing, or name collisions across the codebase.

rules.rule_statement: Identifiers shall be distinct from macro names
rules.description: This code violates Rule 5.5 because identifiers shall be distinct from macro names. Poor identifier hygiene creates confusion, shadowing, or name collisions across the codebase.

rules.rule_statement: A typedef name shall be a unique identifier
rules.description: This code violates Rule 5.6 because a typedef name shall be a unique identifier. Poor identifier hygiene creates confusion, shadowing, or name collisions across the codebase.

rules.rule_statement: A tag name shall be a unique identifier
rules.description: This code violates Rule 5.7 because a tag name shall be a unique identifier. Poor identifier hygiene creates confusion, shadowing, or name collisions across the codebase.

rules.rule_statement: Identifiers that define objects or functions with external linkage shall be unique
rules.description: This code violates Rule 5.8 because identifiers that define objects or functions with external linkage shall be unique. Poor identifier hygiene creates confusion, shadowing, or name collisions across the codebase.

rules.rule_statement: Identifiers that define objects or functions with internal linkage should be unique
rules.description: This code violates Rule 5.9 because identifiers that define objects or functions with internal linkage should be unique. Poor identifier hygiene creates confusion, shadowing, or name collisions across the codebase.

rules.rule_statement: Bit-fields shall only be declared with an appropriate type
rules.description: This code violates Rule 6.1 because bit-fields shall only be declared with an appropriate type. Inappropriate bit-field typing can cause portability and sign-related issues.

rules.rule_statement: Single-bit named bit-fields shall not be of a signed type
rules.description: This code violates Rule 6.2 because single-bit named bit-fields shall not be of a signed type. Inappropriate bit-field typing can cause portability and sign-related issues.

rules.rule_statement: Octal constants shall not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 7.1 because octal constants shall not be used. Literal notation must make the intended type explicit and unambiguous.
rules.violated_code: int mode = 010;   /* octal constant */

rules.rule_statement: A
rules.description: This code violates Rule 7.2 because a "u" or "U" suffix shall be applied to all integer constants that are represented in an unsigned type. Literal notation must make the intended type explicit and unambiguous.
rules.violated_code: unsigned int mask = 1 << 7;   /* missing U suffix */

rules.rule_statement: The lowercase character
rules.description: This code violates Rule 7.3 because the lowercase character "l" shall not be used in a literal suffix. Literal notation must make the intended type explicit and unambiguous.
rules.violated_code: long value = 10l;   /* lowercase l suffix */

rules.rule_statement: A string literal shall not be assigned to an object unless the object type is pointer to const-qualified char
rules.description: This code violates Rule 7.4 because a string literal shall not be assigned to an object unless the object type is pointer to const-qualified char. Literal notation must make the intended type explicit and unambiguous.
rules.violated_code: char *msg = "hello";   /* should be pointer to const-qualified char */

rules.rule_statement: Types shall be explicitly specified
rules.description: This code violates Rule 8.1 because types shall be explicitly specified. Inconsistent declarations and linkage decisions reduce maintainability and can break interface correctness.
rules.violated_code: static count;   /* type not explicitly specified */

rules.rule_statement: Function types shall be in prototype form with named parameters
rules.description: This code violates Rule 8.2 because function types shall be in prototype form with named parameters. Inconsistent declarations and linkage decisions reduce maintainability and can break interface correctness.

rules.rule_statement: All declarations of an object or function shall use the same names and type qualifiers
rules.description: This code violates Rule 8.3 because all declarations of an object or function shall use the same names and type qualifiers. Inconsistent declarations and linkage decisions reduce maintainability and can break interface correctness.
rules.violated_code:
extern const int limit;
extern int limit;   /* qualifiers differ */

rules.rule_statement: A compatible declaration shall be visible when an object or function with external linkage is defined
rules.description: This code violates Rule 8.4 because a compatible declaration shall be visible when an object or function with external linkage is defined. Inconsistent declarations and linkage decisions reduce maintainability and can break interface correctness.

rules.rule_statement: An external object or function shall be declared once in one and only one file
rules.description: This code violates Rule 8.5 because an external object or function shall be declared once in one and only one file. Inconsistent declarations and linkage decisions reduce maintainability and can break interface correctness.

rules.rule_statement: An identifier with external linkage shall have exactly one external definition
rules.description: This code violates Rule 8.6 because an identifier with external linkage shall have exactly one external definition. Inconsistent declarations and linkage decisions reduce maintainability and can break interface correctness.
rules.violated_code:
int status;
int status;   /* multiple external definitions */

rules.rule_statement: Functions and objects should not be defined with external linkage if they are referenced in only one translation unit
rules.description: This code violates Rule 8.7 because functions and objects should not be defined with external linkage if they are referenced in only one translation unit. Inconsistent declarations and linkage decisions reduce maintainability and can break interface correctness.
rules.violated_code: int local_only(void) { return 0; }   /* referenced in one translation unit only */

rules.rule_statement: The static storage class specifier shall be used in all declarations of objects and functions that have internal linkage
rules.description: This code violates Rule 8.8 because the static storage class specifier shall be used in all declarations of objects and functions that have internal linkage. Inconsistent declarations and linkage decisions reduce maintainability and can break interface correctness.
rules.violated_code: int helper(void) { return 0; }   /* should be static if internal linkage intended */

rules.rule_statement: An object should be defined at block scope if its identifier only appears in a single function
rules.description: This code violates Rule 8.9 because an object should be defined at block scope if its identifier only appears in a single function. Inconsistent declarations and linkage decisions reduce maintainability and can break interface correctness.
rules.violated_code: int temp_result;   /* only used inside one function */

rules.rule_statement: An inline function shall be declared with the static storage class
rules.description: This code violates Rule 8.10 because an inline function shall be declared with the static storage class. Inconsistent declarations and linkage decisions reduce maintainability and can break interface correctness.
rules.violated_code: inline int square(int x) { return x * x; }   /* missing static */

rules.rule_statement: The extern keyword shall be used in declarations of objects and functions that have external linkage
rules.description: This code violates Rule 8.11 because the extern keyword shall be used in declarations of objects and functions that have external linkage. Inconsistent declarations and linkage decisions reduce maintainability and can break interface correctness.
rules.violated_code: extern uint8_t buffer[];   /* size omitted */

rules.rule_statement: Within an enumerator list, the value of an implicitly specified enumeration constant shall be unique
rules.description: This code violates Rule 8.12 because within an enumerator list, the value of an implicitly specified enumeration constant shall be unique. Inconsistent declarations and linkage decisions reduce maintainability and can break interface correctness.
rules.violated_code: enum state { IDLE = 0, RUN = 0 };   /* duplicate implicit/explicit value mix causing non-unique value */

rules.rule_statement: A pointer should point to a const-qualified type whenever possible
rules.description: This code violates Rule 8.13 because a pointer should point to a const-qualified type whenever possible. Inconsistent declarations and linkage decisions reduce maintainability and can break interface correctness.

rules.rule_statement: The restrict type qualifier shall not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 8.14 because the restrict type qualifier shall not be used. Inconsistent declarations and linkage decisions reduce maintainability and can break interface correctness.
rules.violated_code: int sum(int * restrict a, int * restrict b);

rules.rule_statement: Non-initialized local variable
rules.description: Variable 'x' is read before being assigned a value. Reading an automatic variable before it is set leads to undefined behaviour and unpredictable results.

rules.rule_statement: Braces shall be used to indicate and match the structure in the non-zero initialization of arrays and structures
rules.description: This code violates Rule 9.2 because braces shall be used to indicate and match the structure in the non-zero initialization of arrays and structures. Incorrect initialization can leave objects in unexpected states and introduce subtle runtime defects.
rules.violated_code: int a[2] = 1, 2;   /* aggregate initializer not enclosed in braces */

rules.rule_statement: Arrays shall not be partially initialized
rules.description: This code violates Rule 9.3 because arrays shall not be partially initialized. Incorrect initialization can leave objects in unexpected states and introduce subtle runtime defects.
rules.violated_code: int a[3] = { 1, 2 };   /* partially initialized array */

rules.rule_statement: An element of an object shall not be initialized more than once
rules.description: This code violates Rule 9.4 because an element of an object shall not be initialized more than once. Incorrect initialization can leave objects in unexpected states and introduce subtle runtime defects.
rules.violated_code: struct point p = { .x = 1, .x = 2 };   /* initialized more than once */

rules.rule_statement: Where designated initializers are used to initialize an array object, the size of the array shall be specified explicitly
rules.description: This code violates Rule 9.5 because where designated initializers are used to initialize an array object, the size of the array shall be specified explicitly. Incorrect initialization can leave objects in unexpected states and introduce subtle runtime defects.
rules.violated_code: int a[] = { [2] = 1 };   /* designated initializer without explicit size */

rules.rule_statement: Operands shall not be of an inappropriate essential type
rules.description: This code violates Rule 10.1 because operands shall not be of an inappropriate essential type. Essential type mismatches can silently change values, truncate data, or alter signedness and behaviour.
rules.violated_code:
uint8_t u = 1U;
if (~u) { }   /* inappropriate essential type use */

rules.rule_statement: Expressions of essentially character type shall not be used inappropriately in addition and subtraction operations
rules.description: This code violates Rule 10.2 because expressions of essentially character type shall not be used inappropriately in addition and subtraction operations. Essential type mismatches can silently change values, truncate data, or alter signedness and behaviour.
rules.violated_code:
char c = 'A';
int x = c + 1;   /* character type used in arithmetic */

rules.rule_statement: Implicit narrowing conversion
rules.description: The value of 'u16val' (uint16_t) is assigned to 'u8val' (uint8_t). This implicit narrowing conversion may silently truncate the value (300 → 44), causing data loss and incorrect program behaviour.

rules.rule_statement: Both operands of an operator in which the usual arithmetic conversions are performed shall have the same essential type category
rules.description: This code violates Rule 10.4 because both operands of an operator in which the usual arithmetic conversions are performed shall have the same essential type category. Essential type mismatches can silently change values, truncate data, or alter signedness and behaviour.

rules.rule_statement: The value of an expression should not be cast to an inappropriate essential type
rules.description: This code violates Rule 10.5 because the value of an expression should not be cast to an inappropriate essential type. Essential type mismatches can silently change values, truncate data, or alter signedness and behaviour.
rules.violated_code:
uint32_t wide = 300U;
uint8_t  small = (uint8_t)wide;   /* cast to inappropriate essential type */

rules.rule_statement: The value of a composite expression shall not be assigned to an object with a wider essential type
rules.description: This code violates Rule 10.6 because the value of a composite expression shall not be assigned to an object with a wider essential type. Essential type mismatches can silently change values, truncate data, or alter signedness and behaviour.

rules.rule_statement: If a composite expression is used as one operand of an operator in which the usual arithmetic conversions are performed, then the other operand shall not have wider essential type
rules.description: This code violates Rule 10.7 because if a composite expression is used as one operand of an operator in which the usual arithmetic conversions are performed, then the other operand shall not have wider essential type. Essential type mismatches can silently change values, truncate data, or alter signedness and behaviour.

rules.rule_statement: The value of a composite expression shall not be cast to a different essential type category or a wider essential type
rules.description: This code violates Rule 10.8 because the value of a composite expression shall not be cast to a different essential type category or a wider essential type. Essential type mismatches can silently change values, truncate data, or alter signedness and behaviour.
rules.violated_code:
uint16_t a, b;
uint32_t x = (uint32_t)(a + b);   /* cast composite expression to wider category */

rules.rule_statement: Conversions shall not be performed between a pointer to a function and any other type
rules.description: This code violates Rule 11.1 because conversions shall not be performed between a pointer to a function and any other type. Unsafe pointer conversions can break aliasing, qualification, and portability assumptions.
rules.violated_code:
void (*fp)(void);
int x = (int)fp;   /* function pointer converted to other type */

rules.rule_statement: Conversions shall not be performed between a pointer to an incomplete type and any other type
rules.description: This code violates Rule 11.2 because conversions shall not be performed between a pointer to an incomplete type and any other type. Unsafe pointer conversions can break aliasing, qualification, and portability assumptions.

rules.rule_statement: A cast shall not be performed between a pointer to object type and a pointer to a different object type
rules.description: This code violates Rule 11.3 because a cast shall not be performed between a pointer to object type and a pointer to a different object type. Unsafe pointer conversions can break aliasing, qualification, and portability assumptions.
rules.violated_code:
int *ip;
float *fp = (float *)ip;   /* object pointer cast to different object type */

rules.rule_statement: A conversion should not be performed between a pointer to object and an integer type
rules.description: This code violates Rule 11.4 because a conversion should not be performed between a pointer to object and an integer type. Unsafe pointer conversions can break aliasing, qualification, and portability assumptions.
rules.violated_code:
int *p;
uintptr_t addr = (uintptr_t)p;   /* pointer-object to integer conversion */

rules.rule_statement: A conversion should not be performed from pointer to void into pointer to object
rules.description: This code violates Rule 11.5 because a conversion should not be performed from pointer to void into pointer to object. Unsafe pointer conversions can break aliasing, qualification, and portability assumptions.
rules.violated_code:
void *vp;
int *ip = (int *)vp;   /* void* to object pointer conversion */

rules.rule_statement: A cast shall not be performed between pointer to void and an arithmetic type
rules.description: This code violates Rule 11.6 because a cast shall not be performed between pointer to void and an arithmetic type. Unsafe pointer conversions can break aliasing, qualification, and portability assumptions.
rules.violated_code: void *vp = (void *)1U;   /* arithmetic type to void pointer */

rules.rule_statement: A cast shall not be performed between pointer to object and a non-integer arithmetic type
rules.description: This code violates Rule 11.7 because a cast shall not be performed between pointer to object and a non-integer arithmetic type. Unsafe pointer conversions can break aliasing, qualification, and portability assumptions.
rules.violated_code:
int *ip;
float32_t f = (float32_t)ip;   /* object pointer to non-integer arithmetic type */

rules.rule_statement: A cast shall not remove any const or volatile qualification from the type pointed to by a pointer
rules.description: This code violates Rule 11.8 because a cast shall not remove any const or volatile qualification from the type pointed to by a pointer. Unsafe pointer conversions can break aliasing, qualification, and portability assumptions.
rules.violated_code:
const int *cp;
int *p = (int *)cp;   /* const removed */

rules.rule_statement: The macro NULL shall be the only permitted form of integer null pointer constant
rules.description: This code violates Rule 11.9 because the macro NULL shall be the only permitted form of integer null pointer constant. Unsafe pointer conversions can break aliasing, qualification, and portability assumptions.
rules.violated_code: int *p = 0;   /* should use NULL */

rules.rule_statement: The precedence of operators within expressions should be made explicit
rules.description: This code violates Rule 12.1 because the precedence of operators within expressions should be made explicit. Expression complexity and unsafe shifts can hide errors and make evaluation harder to reason about.
rules.violated_code: int x = a + b << 1;   /* precedence not made explicit */

rules.rule_statement: The right hand operand of a shift operator shall lie between zero and one less than the width in bits of the essential type of the left hand operand
rules.description: This code violates Rule 12.2 because the right hand operand of a shift operator shall lie between zero and one less than the width in bits of the essential type of the left hand operand. Expression complexity and unsafe shifts can hide errors and make evaluation harder to reason about.
rules.violated_code:
uint8_t x = 1U;
uint8_t y = x << 8;   /* shift count out of range */

rules.rule_statement: The comma operator should not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 12.3 because the comma operator should not be used. Expression complexity and unsafe shifts can hide errors and make evaluation harder to reason about.
rules.violated_code: int x = (a++, b);   /* comma operator */

rules.rule_statement: Evaluation of constant unsigned integer expressions should not wrap around
rules.description: This code violates Rule 12.4 because evaluation of constant unsigned integer expressions should not wrap around. Expression complexity and unsafe shifts can hide errors and make evaluation harder to reason about.
rules.violated_code: const uint8_t x = 255U + 1U;   /* unsigned wrap-around in constant expression */

rules.rule_statement: Initializer lists shall not contain persistent side effects
rules.description: This code violates Rule 13.1 because initializer lists shall not contain persistent side effects. Side effects in complex expressions make behaviour harder to predict and verify.
rules.violated_code:
int i = 0;
int a[2] = { i++, i };   /* persistent side effect in initializer */

rules.rule_statement: The value of an expression and its persistent side effects shall be the same under all permitted evaluation orders
rules.description: This code violates Rule 13.2 because the value of an expression and its persistent side effects shall be the same under all permitted evaluation orders. Side effects in complex expressions make behaviour harder to predict and verify.
rules.violated_code: a[i] = i++;   /* value depends on evaluation order */

rules.rule_statement: A full expression containing an increment or decrement operator should have no other potential side effects other than that caused by the increment or decrement operator
rules.description: This code violates Rule 13.3 because a full expression containing an increment or decrement operator should have no other potential side effects other than that caused by the increment or decrement operator. Side effects in complex expressions make behaviour harder to predict and verify.
rules.violated_code: x = y++ + z;   /* ++ mixed with other side effects */

rules.rule_statement: The result of an assignment operator should not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 13.4 because the result of an assignment operator should not be used. Side effects in complex expressions make behaviour harder to predict and verify.
rules.violated_code: if ((x = f()) != 0) { }   /* result of assignment used */

rules.rule_statement: The right hand operand of a logical && or || operator shall not contain persistent side effects
rules.description: This code violates Rule 13.5 because the right hand operand of a logical && or || operator shall not contain persistent side effects. Side effects in complex expressions make behaviour harder to predict and verify.
rules.violated_code: if ((ptr != NULL) && (i++ < 10)) { }   /* side effect in RHS of logical operator */

rules.rule_statement: Side effects in sizeof operand
rules.description: The operand of sizeof contains the expression 'a++' which has a side effect. The sizeof operator does not evaluate its operand at run-time, so the increment never happens, causing silent logic errors.

rules.rule_statement: A loop counter shall not have essentially floating type
rules.description: This code violates Rule 14.1 because a loop counter shall not have essentially floating type. Loop and condition forms should make control flow explicit, safe, and reviewable.

rules.rule_statement: A for loop shall be well-formed
rules.description: This code violates Rule 14.2 because a for loop shall be well-formed. Loop and condition forms should make control flow explicit, safe, and reviewable.
rules.violated_code: for (i = 0, j = 0; i < 10; ++i, ++j) { }   /* not well-formed */

rules.rule_statement: Controlling expressions shall not be invariant
rules.description: This code violates Rule 14.3 because controlling expressions shall not be invariant. Loop and condition forms should make control flow explicit, safe, and reviewable.
rules.violated_code: while (1) { task(); }   /* invariant controlling expression */

rules.rule_statement: Non-Boolean if-controlling expression
rules.description: The controlling expression of the 'if' statement is 'count', an integer, not an essentially Boolean type. Using non-Boolean values as conditions reduces readability and may mask logic errors where the value is non-zero but not the intended condition.

rules.rule_statement: The goto statement should not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 15.1 because the goto statement should not be used. Unstructured control flow makes code harder to reason about and maintain.

rules.rule_statement: The goto statement shall jump to a label declared later in the same function
rules.description: This code violates Rule 15.2 because the goto statement shall jump to a label declared later in the same function. Unstructured control flow makes code harder to reason about and maintain.

rules.rule_statement: Any label referenced by a goto statement shall be declared in the same block, or in any block enclosing the goto statement
rules.description: This code violates Rule 15.3 because any label referenced by a goto statement shall be declared in the same block, or in any block enclosing the goto statement. Unstructured control flow makes code harder to reason about and maintain.

rules.rule_statement: There should be no more than one break or goto statement used to terminate any iteration statement
rules.description: This code violates Rule 15.4 because there should be no more than one break or goto statement used to terminate any iteration statement. Unstructured control flow makes code harder to reason about and maintain.

rules.rule_statement: A function should have a single point of exit at the end
rules.description: This code violates Rule 15.5 because a function should have a single point of exit at the end. Unstructured control flow makes code harder to reason about and maintain.

rules.rule_statement: The body of an iteration statement or selection statement shall be a compound statement
rules.description: This code violates Rule 15.6 because the body of an iteration statement or selection statement shall be a compound statement. Unstructured control flow makes code harder to reason about and maintain.

rules.rule_statement: All if ... else if constructs shall be terminated with an else statement
rules.description: This code violates Rule 15.7 because all if ... else if constructs shall be terminated with an else statement. Unstructured control flow makes code harder to reason about and maintain.

rules.rule_statement: All switch statements shall be well-formed
rules.description: This code violates Rule 16.1 because all switch statements shall be well-formed. Switch logic must be complete, explicit, and easy to follow.
rules.violated_code: switch (x) case 1: y++;   /* malformed switch */

rules.rule_statement: A switch label shall only be used when the most closely-enclosing compound statement is the body of a switch statement
rules.description: This code violates Rule 16.2 because a switch label shall only be used when the most closely-enclosing compound statement is the body of a switch statement. Switch logic must be complete, explicit, and easy to follow.
rules.violated_code: case 1: y++;   /* switch label outside switch body */

rules.rule_statement: An unconditional break statement shall terminate every switch-clause
rules.description: This code violates Rule 16.3 because an unconditional break statement shall terminate every switch-clause. Switch logic must be complete, explicit, and easy to follow.
rules.violated_code: switch (x) { case 1: y++; case 2: z++; break; }

rules.rule_statement: Every switch statement shall have a default label
rules.description: This code violates Rule 16.4 because every switch statement shall have a default label. Switch logic must be complete, explicit, and easy to follow.
rules.violated_code: switch (x) { case 0: y++; break; }   /* no default */

rules.rule_statement: A default label shall appear either as the first or the last switch label of a switch statement
rules.description: This code violates Rule 16.5 because a default label shall appear either as the first or the last switch label of a switch statement. Switch logic must be complete, explicit, and easy to follow.
rules.violated_code: switch (x) { case 0: break; default: break; case 1: break; }

rules.rule_statement: Every switch statement shall have at least two switch-clauses
rules.description: This code violates Rule 16.6 because every switch statement shall have at least two switch-clauses. Switch logic must be complete, explicit, and easy to follow.
rules.violated_code: switch (x) { default: break; }   /* only one switch-clause */

rules.rule_statement: A switch expression shall not have essentially Boolean type
rules.description: This code violates Rule 16.7 because a switch expression shall not have essentially Boolean type. Switch logic must be complete, explicit, and easy to follow.
rules.violated_code:
bool flag;
switch (flag) { case 0: break; default: break; }

rules.rule_statement: The standard header file <stdarg.h> shall not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 17.1 because the standard header file <stdarg.h> shall not be used. Function interfaces and calls must be explicit, safe, and consistently used.
rules.violated_code: int log_values(int count, ...);   /* stdarg feature */

rules.rule_statement: Functions shall not call themselves, either directly or indirectly
rules.description: This code violates Rule 17.2 because functions shall not call themselves, either directly or indirectly. Function interfaces and calls must be explicit, safe, and consistently used.
rules.violated_code: int fact(int n) { return (n <= 1) ? 1 : n * fact(n - 1); }

rules.rule_statement: A function shall not be declared implicitly
rules.description: This code violates Rule 17.3 because a function shall not be declared implicitly. Function interfaces and calls must be explicit, safe, and consistently used.

rules.rule_statement: All exit paths from a function with non-void return type shall have an explicit return statement with an expression
rules.description: This code violates Rule 17.4 because all exit paths from a function with non-void return type shall have an explicit return statement with an expression. Function interfaces and calls must be explicit, safe, and consistently used.

rules.rule_statement: The function argument corresponding to a parameter declared to have an array type shall have an appropriate number of elements
rules.description: This code violates Rule 17.5 because the function argument corresponding to a parameter declared to have an array type shall have an appropriate number of elements. Function interfaces and calls must be explicit, safe, and consistently used.

rules.rule_statement: The declaration of an array parameter shall not contain the static keyword between the [ ]
rules.description: This code violates Rule 17.6 because the declaration of an array parameter shall not contain the static keyword between the [ ]. Function interfaces and calls must be explicit, safe, and consistently used.
rules.violated_code: void f(uint8_t buf[static 4]);

rules.rule_statement: The value returned by a function having non-void return type shall be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 17.7 because the value returned by a function having non-void return type shall be used. Function interfaces and calls must be explicit, safe, and consistently used.
rules.violated_code: compute_crc(data, len);   /* return value ignored */

rules.rule_statement: A function parameter should not be modified
rules.description: This code violates Rule 17.8 because a function parameter should not be modified. Function interfaces and calls must be explicit, safe, and consistently used.

rules.rule_statement: A pointer resulting from arithmetic on a pointer operand shall address an element of the same array as that pointer operand
rules.description: This code violates Rule 18.1 because a pointer resulting from arithmetic on a pointer operand shall address an element of the same array as that pointer operand. Pointer lifetime and pointer arithmetic mistakes can easily produce undefined behaviour.

rules.rule_statement: Subtraction between pointers shall only be applied to pointers that address elements of the same array
rules.description: This code violates Rule 18.2 because subtraction between pointers shall only be applied to pointers that address elements of the same array. Pointer lifetime and pointer arithmetic mistakes can easily produce undefined behaviour.
rules.violated_code:
int a[4], b[4];
ptrdiff_t d = &a[1] - &b[1];

rules.rule_statement: The relational operators > >= < <= shall not be applied to objects of pointer type except where they point into the same array
rules.description: This code violates Rule 18.3 because the relational operators > >= < <= shall not be applied to objects of pointer type except where they point into the same array. Pointer lifetime and pointer arithmetic mistakes can easily produce undefined behaviour.

rules.rule_statement: The +, -, += and -= operators should not be applied to an expression of pointer type
rules.description: This code violates Rule 18.4 because the +, -, += and -= operators should not be applied to an expression of pointer type. Pointer lifetime and pointer arithmetic mistakes can easily produce undefined behaviour.
rules.violated_code: p = p + 1;   /* pointer arithmetic */

rules.rule_statement: Declarations should contain no more than two levels of pointer nesting
rules.description: This code violates Rule 18.5 because declarations should contain no more than two levels of pointer nesting. Pointer lifetime and pointer arithmetic mistakes can easily produce undefined behaviour.
rules.violated_code: int ***ppp;   /* more than two levels of pointer nesting */

rules.rule_statement: The address of an object with automatic storage shall not be copied to another object that persists after the first object has ceased to exist
rules.description: This code violates Rule 18.6 because the address of an object with automatic storage shall not be copied to another object that persists after the first object has ceased to exist. Pointer lifetime and pointer arithmetic mistakes can easily produce undefined behaviour.

rules.rule_statement: Flexible array members shall not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 18.7 because flexible array members shall not be used. Pointer lifetime and pointer arithmetic mistakes can easily produce undefined behaviour.
rules.violated_code: struct packet { uint16_t len; uint8_t data[]; };

rules.rule_statement: Variable-length arrays shall not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 18.8 because variable-length arrays shall not be used. Pointer lifetime and pointer arithmetic mistakes can easily produce undefined behaviour.

rules.rule_statement: An object shall not be assigned or copied to an overlapping object
rules.description: This code violates Rule 19.1 because an object shall not be assigned or copied to an overlapping object. Overlapping or unsafe object manipulation can corrupt data and invoke undefined behaviour.
rules.violated_code: memcpy(&a[0], &a[1], 4U);   /* overlapping copy */

rules.rule_statement: The union keyword should not be used C90 [Undefined 39, 40; Implementation 27],
rules.description: This code violates Rule 19.2 because the union keyword should not be used C90 [Undefined 39, 40; Implementation 27],. Overlapping or unsafe object manipulation can corrupt data and invoke undefined behaviour.
rules.violated_code: union value_u { uint32_t u32; float32_t f32; };

rules.rule_statement: #include directives should only be preceded by preprocessor directives or comments
rules.description: This code violates Rule 20.1 because #include directives should only be preceded by preprocessor directives or comments. Preprocessor misuse can obscure intent and create fragile expansions.
rules.violated_code:
int x = 0;
#include "cfg.h"   /* include not at start with only pp/comment before it */

rules.rule_statement: The #include directive shall be followed by either a <filename> or
rules.description: This code violates Rule 20.2 because the #include directive shall be followed by either a <filename> or "filename" sequence. Preprocessor misuse can obscure intent and create fragile expansions.
rules.violated_code: #include "my\header.h"

rules.rule_statement: The #include directive shall be followed by either a <filename> or
rules.description: This code violates Rule 20.3 because the #include directive shall be followed by either a <filename> or "filename" sequence. Preprocessor misuse can obscure intent and create fragile expansions.
rules.violated_code: #include MY_HEADER   /* not <file> or "file" */

rules.rule_statement: A macro shall not be defined with the same name as a keyword
rules.description: This code violates Rule 20.4 because a macro shall not be defined with the same name as a keyword. Preprocessor misuse can obscure intent and create fragile expansions.
rules.violated_code: #define for(x)  (x)

rules.rule_statement: #undef should not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 20.5 because #undef should not be used. Preprocessor misuse can obscure intent and create fragile expansions.
rules.violated_code: #undef LIMIT

rules.rule_statement: Tokens that look like a preprocessing directive shall not occur within a macro argument
rules.description: This code violates Rule 20.6 because tokens that look like a preprocessing directive shall not occur within a macro argument. Preprocessor misuse can obscure intent and create fragile expansions.
rules.violated_code:
#define F(x) x
F(#include)

rules.rule_statement: Expressions resulting from the expansion of macro parameters shall be enclosed in parentheses
rules.description: This code violates Rule 20.7 because expressions resulting from the expansion of macro parameters shall be enclosed in parentheses. Preprocessor misuse can obscure intent and create fragile expansions.
rules.violated_code:
#define ADD(x,y) x + y
int z = ADD(a,b) * c;

rules.rule_statement: The controlling expression of a #if or #elif preprocessing directive shall evaluate to 0 or 1
rules.description: This code violates Rule 20.8 because the controlling expression of a #if or #elif preprocessing directive shall evaluate to 0 or 1. Preprocessor misuse can obscure intent and create fragile expansions.
rules.violated_code:
#if VALUE + 2
#endif

rules.rule_statement: All identifiers used in the controlling expression of #if or #elif shall be #define'd before evaluation
rules.description: This code violates Rule 20.9 because all identifiers used in the controlling expression of #if or #elif shall be #define'd before evaluation. Preprocessor misuse can obscure intent and create fragile expansions.
rules.violated_code:
#if FEATURE_FLAG
#endif   /* identifier not defined before use */

rules.rule_statement: The # and ## preprocessor operators should not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 20.10 because the # and ## preprocessor operators should not be used. Preprocessor misuse can obscure intent and create fragile expansions.
rules.violated_code: #define JOIN(a,b) a ## b

rules.rule_statement: A macro parameter immediately following a # operator should not be subject to further macro replacement
rules.description: This code violates Rule 20.11 because a macro parameter immediately following a # operator should not be subject to further macro replacement. Preprocessor misuse can obscure intent and create fragile expansions.
rules.violated_code: #define STRCAT(x) #x ## suffix

rules.rule_statement: A macro parameter used as an operand to the # or ## operators, which is itself subject to further macro replacement, shall only be used as an operand to these operators
rules.description: This code violates Rule 20.12 because a macro parameter used as an operand to the # or ## operators, which is itself subject to further macro replacement, shall only be used as an operand to these operators. Preprocessor misuse can obscure intent and create fragile expansions.
rules.violated_code:
#define M(x) #x
#define ID y
M(ID)   /* operand subject to further replacement */

rules.rule_statement: A line whose first token is # shall be a valid preprocessing directive
rules.description: This code violates Rule 20.13 because a line whose first token is # shall be a valid preprocessing directive. Preprocessor misuse can obscure intent and create fragile expansions.
rules.violated_code: # bad_directive

rules.rule_statement: All #else, #elif and #endif preprocessor directives shall reside in the same file a s the #if, #ifdefor #ifndef directive to which they are related
rules.description: This code violates Rule 20.14 because all #else, #elif and #endif preprocessor directives shall reside in the same file a s the #if, #ifdefor #ifndef directive to which they are related. Preprocessor misuse can obscure intent and create fragile expansions.

rules.rule_statement: #define and #undef shall not be used on a reserved identifier or reserved macro name
rules.description: This code violates Rule 21.1 because #define and #undef shall not be used on a reserved identifier or reserved macro name. Restricted standard-library features are disallowed because they reduce determinism, analyzability, or portability.
rules.violated_code: #define __LINE__  10

rules.rule_statement: A reserved identifier or macro name shall not be declared
rules.description: This code violates Rule 21.2 because a reserved identifier or macro name shall not be declared. Restricted standard-library features are disallowed because they reduce determinism, analyzability, or portability.
rules.violated_code: int errno;   /* reserved identifier declared */

rules.rule_statement: The memory allocation and deallocation functions of <stdlib.h> shall not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 21.3 because the memory allocation and deallocation functions of <stdlib.h> shall not be used. Restricted standard-library features are disallowed because they reduce determinism, analyzability, or portability.
rules.violated_code: void *p = malloc(16U);

rules.rule_statement: The standard header file <setjmp.h> shall not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 21.4 because the standard header file <setjmp.h> shall not be used. Restricted standard-library features are disallowed because they reduce determinism, analyzability, or portability.
rules.violated_code: #include <setjmp.h>

rules.rule_statement: The standard signal handling facilities of <signal.h> shall not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 21.5 because the standard signal handling facilities of <signal.h> shall not be used. Restricted standard-library features are disallowed because they reduce determinism, analyzability, or portability.
rules.violated_code: #include <signal.h>

rules.rule_statement: The standard input/output library <stdio.h> shall not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 21.6 because the standard input/output library <stdio.h> shall not be used. Restricted standard-library features are disallowed because they reduce determinism, analyzability, or portability.
rules.violated_code: printf("value=%d\n", x);

rules.rule_statement: The atof, atoi, atol and atoll functions of <stdlib.h> shall not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 21.7 because the atof, atoi, atol and atoll functions of <stdlib.h> shall not be used. Restricted standard-library features are disallowed because they reduce determinism, analyzability, or portability.
rules.violated_code: int n = atoi(text);

rules.rule_statement: The abort, exit, getenv and system functions of <stdlib.h> shall not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 21.8 because the abort, exit, getenv and system functions of <stdlib.h> shall not be used. Restricted standard-library features are disallowed because they reduce determinism, analyzability, or portability.
rules.violated_code: system("dir");

rules.rule_statement: The bsearch function of <stdlib.h> shall not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 21.9 because the bsearch function of <stdlib.h> shall not be used. Restricted standard-library features are disallowed because they reduce determinism, analyzability, or portability.
rules.violated_code: qsort(a, n, sizeof(a[0]), cmp);

rules.rule_statement: The time and date functions of <time.h> shall not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 21.10 because the time and date functions of <time.h> shall not be used. Restricted standard-library features are disallowed because they reduce determinism, analyzability, or portability.
rules.violated_code: time_t now = time(NULL);

rules.rule_statement: The standard character handling functions of <ctype.h> shall not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 21.11 because the standard character handling functions of <ctype.h> shall not be used. Restricted standard-library features are disallowed because they reduce determinism, analyzability, or portability.
rules.violated_code: #include <tgmath.h>

rules.rule_statement: The exception handling functions of <fenv.h> shall not be used
rules.description: This code violates Rule 21.12 because the exception handling functions of <fenv.h> shall not be used. Restricted standard-library features are disallowed because they reduce determinism, analyzability, or portability.
rules.violated_code:
#include <fenv.h>
fetestexcept(FE_OVERFLOW);

rules.rule_statement: All resources obtained dynamically by means of Standard Library functions shall be explicitly released
rules.description: This code violates Rule 22.1 because all resources obtained dynamically by means of Standard Library functions shall be explicitly released. Resource and stream handling errors can leak resources or use invalid handles.
rules.violated_code: FILE *fp = fopen("tmp", "r");   /* never closed */

rules.rule_statement: A block of memory shall only be freed if it was allocated by means of a Standard Library function
rules.description: This code violates Rule 22.2 because a block of memory shall only be freed if it was allocated by means of a Standard Library function. Resource and stream handling errors can leak resources or use invalid handles.
rules.violated_code:
int x;
free(&x);   /* freeing non-allocated memory */

rules.rule_statement: The same file shall not be open for read and write access at the same time on different streams
rules.description: This code violates Rule 22.3 because the same file shall not be open for read and write access at the same time on different streams. Resource and stream handling errors can leak resources or use invalid handles.
rules.violated_code:
FILE *fw = fopen("tmp", "r+");
FILE *fr = fopen("tmp", "r");

rules.rule_statement: There shall be no attempt to write to a stream which has been opened as read-only
rules.description: This code violates Rule 22.4 because there shall be no attempt to write to a stream which has been opened as read-only. Resource and stream handling errors can leak resources or use invalid handles.
rules.violated_code:
FILE *fp = fopen("tmp", "r");
fprintf(fp, "x");

rules.rule_statement: A pointer to a FILE object shall not be dereferenced
rules.description: This code violates Rule 22.5 because a pointer to a FILE object shall not be dereferenced. Resource and stream handling errors can leak resources or use invalid handles.
rules.violated_code:
FILE *fp;
FILE copy = *fp;   /* FILE object dereferenced */

rules.rule_statement: The value of a pointer to a FILE shall not be used after the associated stream has been closed
rules.description: This code violates Rule 22.6 because the value of a pointer to a FILE shall not be used after the associated stream has been closed. Resource and stream handling errors can leak resources or use invalid handles.
